items.title: AD Feedback
items.body:
Area Director feedback from Deb Cooley: https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/oauth/_ujmJ_Gdl0kCmQzau4fskRwT3B4/

Repeated below for convenience:

---------------------------------------
General:  Consider using *.*.example.org for example domains (currently
this draft uses [as.*.org](http://as.*.org/))  See:  https://authors.ietf.org/example-addresses
 for more information.

Long line:  Line 416, is 75 characters long, according to idnits
(experimental).  I did not go looking for this (which is why I've put it up
front).

Section 2.1, Appendix B:  Using steps (A), (B), (C) could be confusing in
light of the pre-existing domain enumerations of Domain A, Domain B.  I'd
suggest using some other enumeration for the steps, maybe (1), (2), (3) to
disambiguate.

Section 2.2:  Since this 'MAY' does not enable/disable interoperability
(the goal of BCP 14), consider changing it to 'may' or even 'could'.

Section 2.3, bullet 1:  'MUST deny' or 'MUST NOT approve'?  Which will be
more obvious to the implementer?

Section 2.4.3:  There is an assumption here that the previous
rules/steps/checks have been validated without error.  Perhaps consider
adding a phrase like, 'When the authorization grant has been validated,
the....'

Section 5:  BCP14 language or not?  Please see:
https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/statement-iesg-statement-on-clarifying-the-use-of-bcp-14-key-words/
. While BCP 14 words in Security Considerations is completely appropriate,
it should still be used sparingly.  The word 'must' is just as normative as
'MUST', but carries less baggage.  I would suggest going back through
Section 5, and consider which of the BCP 14 words are there for
'interoperability' or to 'prevent harm'.  Please pay special attention to
section on 'SHOULD' and 'RECOMMENDED'.  I'm making some comments within
Section 5 to emphasize my point of view.

Section 5.1:  Naked 'SHOULD's need to have a 'what are the consequences of
violating the should' that goes with it.  An alternative is to state under
what reasonable circumstances the should isn't possible (maybe that is the
public client part of this section?).

Section 5.4:  I'm guessing that there was confusion at some point about
issuance of refresh tokens by AS domain A to clients in domain A?  My
suggestion to make this a tiny bit clearer in para 1 is to add 'domain A'
when you mention 'client' -> 'client in domain A'.  It does make it wordier
and longer, but way more obvious that the refresh tokens across domains are
the issue.

Section 5.4: It is also not clear why one might choose to violate the
sequence of 'SHOULD NOT', 'SHOULD', 'SHOULD'...  Why not 'MUST NOT',
'MUST', 'MUST'?  Sadly, RFC 7521 doesn't specify why/when 'SHOULD NOT'
could be ignored (unless I read it incorrectly).

Section 5.5:  Why not 'MUST'?  Seems reasonable to say that one MUST
evaluate the risk and since there is no definition of 'appropriate', then
any mitigations will do, no?

IANA Considerations:  Add this, see:
https://authors.ietf.org/en/iana-considerations .  It *might* be possible
to remove it prior to publication.  IANA will know (and tell you) for sure.

Acknowledgements (Appendix C):  I would remove the parenthetical and just
state a generic 'and others for their valuable input....'.  Naming everyone
who had input is not actually required.  [rationale:  the () won't age
well]

items.title: Updates to reflect changes to RFC7523 (jwt_privatekey attack)
items.body: Remove reference to token endpoint in JWT authorization grant section to protect against malicious authorization servers (see https://github.com/selfissued/draft-jones-oauth-rfc7523bis )

items.title: Question: DPoP proof handling across trust domain boundaries
items.body:
## Question

draft-ietf-oauth-identity-chaining mentions DPoP in Appendix B.3 but I'm 
trying to understand the intended behavior for a specific scenario.

## Scenario

Multi-hop authentication where:
1. User authenticates to Service A with DPoP-bound token
2. Service A needs to exchange that token with Domain B's AS
3. New token should be DPoP-bound to Service A's key

## The htu binding question

When the user authenticated to Service A, their DPoP proof had:
```
{ "htu": "https://service-a.example.com/auth", "htm": "POST" }
```

If Service A wants to prove to Domain B's AS that the user's DPoP was
verified, what's the expected mechanism?

Options I'm considering:

**(A)** Service A forwards user's original proof
- Problem: htu doesn't match Domain B's token endpoint

**(B)** Service A attests "I verified the user's DPoP"  
- Problem: Domain B must trust Service A's assertion

**(C)** User pre-generates proof for Domain B's endpoint
- Problem: User must know exchange flow in advance

## Questions

1. Is there a recommended approach for this scenario, or is it intentionally
   left to implementers?
2. If it's left to implementers, are there any pitfalls to avoid?

Happy to help if this is something the WG wants to address.

---

**References**:
- [Mailing list: DPoP for token exchange](https://www.mail-archive.com/oauth@ietf.org/msg24953.html)
- [Appendix B.3 - Delegated Key Binding](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-oauth-identity-chaining#appendix-B.3)

items.title: Add `authorization_grant_profiles_supported` to client metadata too
items.body:
Clients should be able to advertise support for this profile via DCR/CIMD as well.

This would need a new section "Client Metadata" after "Authorization Server Metadata", and a new IANA registry section to add `authorization_grant_profiles_supported` and `urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-profile:id-jag` to the [DCR registry](https://www.iana.org/assignments/oauth-parameters/oauth-parameters.xhtml#client-metadata).

items.title: Adding Optional `actor_token` to ID-JAG for Explicit Actor Modeling
items.body:
# Adding Optional `actor_token` to ID-JAG for Explicit Actor Modeling

## Summary

The OAuth Identity Assertion Authorization Grant (ID-JAG) deliberately enforces **client continuity**:

- the `subject_token` was issued to a specific OAuth client
- that same client (`client_id`) MUST present the `subject_token` when requesting the ID-JAG

This is a strong and desirable property. It prevents cross-client substitution and ensures that the OAuth client that obtained the upstream subject credential is the one continuing the flow.

However, client continuity and actor identity are not the same thing.

Client continuity answers:

> Which OAuth client continued the flow?

Many delegated systems also need to answer:

> Which actor actually exercised the subject’s authorization?

In simple deployments, those two identities may coincide. In more complex deployments, they often do not.

This issue proposes adding **optional support for an `actor_token`** so that deployments that need explicit actor fidelity can represent it directly, while preserving ID-JAG’s current client-continuity guarantees.

## Current Model: Client Continuity as Implicit Actor

ID-JAG today enforces:

```text
subject_token -> issued to client A
ID-JAG request -> must be made by client A
```

This guarantees:

> the same OAuth client that received the subject token is the one continuing the authorization flow

That is an important security property because it:
- prevents one client from replaying another client’s subject credential
- preserves the RP/client binding of the original identity assertion
- provides a simple and robust model for traditional RP-to-API scenarios

In effect, the current design treats the authenticated client as the implicit actor.

## The Gap: Client Continuity Is Not Actor Fidelity

The current model proves continuity of the OAuth client. It does not necessarily prove fidelity of the acting principal.

That distinction matters because `client_id` identifies an OAuth client registration, not necessarily the execution principal that is acting on behalf of the subject at runtime.

In modern systems, a single client may front:
- multiple services
- multiple workloads
- multiple agent instances
- multiple execution environments
- both human-initiated and automated execution paths

In those architectures, the current model guarantees:

> client A continued the flow

but it does not guarantee:

> actor X, rather than actor Y, actually exercised the subject’s authorization

### Where the Current Model Falls Short

### 1. Multiple Actors Behind a Single Client

A single `client_id` may represent multiple distinct acting entities.

Example:

```text
client_id = "frontend-app"

frontend-app
  -> orchestration layer
     -> agent A executes task
     -> agent B executes task
```

Both requests:
- use the same `client_id`
- satisfy the current ID-JAG continuity rules

But:
- actor A and actor B are not the same principal
- they may differ in trust level, permissions, or execution context
- downstream services cannot distinguish them if only client identity is preserved

### 2. Delegation Within the Same Client Boundary

A user may initiate a flow through an RP, and an automated agent may later continue it under the same client registration.

From the current ID-JAG model, nothing has changed:
- same `client_id`
- same flow continuity

But operationally:
- the acting entity has changed
- the security, audit, and authorization semantics have changed
- downstream systems may need to know that the request is now being executed by an agent rather than by the original front-end context

#### 3. Loss of Actor Context for Downstream Consumers

Without an explicit actor input, downstream components often see only:
- the issued token
- the client identity carried or inferred
- deployment-specific local context

They do not reliably learn:
- which execution principal acted
- whether a distinct delegated actor was involved
- how to represent that actor across trust boundaries

As a result, actor semantics become deployment-local rather than portable.

### 4. Overloading `client_id`

Using `client_id` as a stand-in for the actor conflates distinct concepts:

- OAuth client identity
- runtime actor identity
- delegated execution identity

That overloading works only when those identities happen to collapse to the same principal. When they diverge, the protocol has no explicit way to express the difference.

## Why This Matters

This is not only an observability issue. It affects:

- **authorization correctness**: policies may need to distinguish subject, client, and actor
- **audit fidelity**: logs should identify the actual delegated actor, not only the client registration
- **interoperability**: actor semantics cannot be carried cleanly across issuers if they are only inferred from local client context
- **security analysis**: confused-deputy and delegation review are harder when actor identity is implicit

See https://mcguinness.github.io/draft-mcguinness-oauth-actor-profile/draft-mcguinness-oauth-actor-profile.html for more discussion on Actor Chaining and Implicit Delegation

## Proposal: Optional `actor_token`

Define an optional request parameter:

```text
actor_token
```

The `actor_token` would:
- represent the entity acting on behalf of the subject
- be validated by the Authorization Server
- allow the AS to embed explicit actor information in the resulting JWT, for example via `act` per https://mcguinness.github.io/draft-mcguinness-oauth-actor-profile/draft-mcguinness-oauth-actor-profile.html

This would not replace the current client-continuity requirement. Rather:

- client continuity would continue to prove that the same OAuth client is continuing the flow
- `actor_token` would optionally provide explicit evidence of which actor is exercising the subject’s authorization

## Why Optional Support Is the Right Shape

This should be optional because:

- existing ID-JAG deployments should continue to work unchanged
- many traditional RP-based flows do not need additional actor modeling
- explicit actor representation is most valuable in multi-actor, multi-hop, or cross-domain delegated systems

Optional `actor_token` support would therefore:
- preserve today’s security model
- avoid imposing new complexity on simple deployments
- enable explicit actor semantics where they are actually needed

## Benefits of Optional `actor_token`

Optional `actor_token` support would:

- preserve the current same-client continuity guarantee
- add first-class actor identity when client continuity is not sufficient
- reduce reliance on deployment-specific inference from `client_id`
- enable downstream tokens to carry explicit actor semantics
- improve audit and authorization fidelity in multi-actor systems
- better support delegated and cross-domain architectures

## Key Question

Client continuity answers which OAuth client continued the flow.

Should ID-JAG also support an optional, explicit mechanism for identifying which actor exercised the subject’s authorization when those are not the same principal?

items.title: Clarity in preconditions for LLM Agent using Enterprise Tools section
items.body:
The `LLM Agent using Enterprise Tools` section preconditions refer to the `External Tool Application` where I believe they should refer to the `SaaS Authorization Server`. This would better align with the structure established in the `Enterprise Deployment` section, which uses `Resource Authorization Server` throughout.

> The LLM Agent has a registered OAuth 2.0 Client (4960880b83dc9) with the External Tool Application (saas.example.net)

The OAuth 2.0 Client is registered at the `SaaS Authorization Server (authorization-server.saas.com)`, not the `External Tool Application`.


> Enterprise has established a trust relationship between their IdP and the External Tool Application for SSO and Identity Assertion JWT Authorization Grant

I believe the trust relationship is established between the IdP and the SaaS Authorization Server instead.

> Enterprise has granted the LLM Agent permission to act on behalf of users for the External Tool Application with a specific set of scopes

This one I am not as sure on but it does not match the structure established in the `Enterprise Deployment Preconditions` section, which refers to the `Resource Authorization Server` here.

items.title: Add recommendations/considerations for user provisioning
items.body:
In the flow proposed by this draft, it is possible for a client to send a token exchange request concerning a user that the resource authorization server has never seen before, e.g. (using the terminology in 4.1) if I try to connect the chat app to the wiki app using my enterprise IdP without ever having created a chat app account.

What should happen in this scenario? I see a few options:
1. The chat app should take the ID-JAG token subject to the IdP and JIT provision a user based on the IdP's response. I think this would lead to a somewhat meaningless grant -- if I don't have a chat app account, what exactly would the wiki app gain from this anyway? Also, depending on the chat app's UX, this might spring a whole user onboarding flow on the user in the middle of the authz request.
2. The chat app should return a user not found error to the wiki app. I think this makes the most sense.
3. The IdP should not even issue an ID-JAG if it knows the user doesn't have a chat app account. This relies on the IdP always knowing whether the account exists, with high confidence, so I'm not sure it's a viable option.

In any case, I think it'd be helpful for the draft to address this scenario.

items.title: Potential Usecase: Whitelabeling of SaaS Services
items.body:
I have a variation of the usecases described in sections `A.2` and `A.3` that may be interesting to talk through. I think authentication for whitelabled SaaS components could be a strong application of XAA. There are other less standardized approaches for this problem today.

----

## Intro

SaaS platforms often purchase and re-expose whitelabeled capabilities from other SaaS platforms. For example,
functionality like comments sections, chat widgets, support bots, or email notifications can often be sold as standalone features and embedded into customer-facing applications. 

This functionality may consist of UI elements, APIs, or the combination of both to create full-stack widgets. When an end user interacts with one of these widgets,it is advantageous to use OAuth access tokens to authorize the interaction so the widget understand the user identity. For example, a comments section widget might allow users to edit their own comment, but not the comments of other users. A feedback widget might want to aggregate feedback by user, or might want to only accept feedback from authenticated users.

Today, there are a few common approaches for whitelabeled authentication usecases:

- Completely unauthenticated widgets like Google Maps use [public API Keys](https://developers.google.com/maps/documentation/javascript/demo-key). These public keys are highly abusable. Attackers that scrape the key can use it to exhaust API quotas and drive up usage bills. No calls within the Google Maps system can be tied back to an end user identity.

- Svix does away with OAuth and access tokens entirely, and has the website backend generate a [pre-signed URL](https://docs.svix.com/app-portal#giving-your-users-access) for the frontend to use. This method is more secure than a completely public API, but still loses the user identity context. 

- The whitelabel resource server could be preconfigured to accept tokens issued by the CIAM IDP directly - like how Supabase can accept access tokens [issued by Auth0](https://supabase.com/docs/guides/auth/third-party/auth0). This approach typically only works with JWT access tokens that adhere to a specific & interoperable format. Access tokens in a bespoke or nonstandard format are much more difficult to validate. This approach also pushes a lot of complexity on the resource server - which may accept access tokens from hundreds or thousands of ASs.

I believe XAA can be used here instead to create a standardized approach.

----

## Applying XAA

Let's take the `wiki.example` case and assume that the wiki app developers want to whitelabel a feedback widget to 
collect feedback from authenticated users on each page. 

This flow differs from other examples already documented in the following ways:
- The IDP Authorization Server is the CIAM AS, operated by the application developer
- The application developer is the persona setting policy considerations
- The client is a first-party application to the IDP AS
- The client is integrated with both the IDP AS and the Resource Server AS - no dynamic discovery of AS information is needed during the flow.

We have the following entities in the system:

```
  +======================+================================+================================================+
  | Role                 | App URL                        | Description                                    |
  +======================+================================+================================================+
  | Client               | https://wiki.example           | Wiki app that embeds content from one or more  |
  |                      |                                | resource servers                               |
  +----------------------+--------------------------------+------------------------------------------------+
  | Identity Provider    | https://auth.wiki.example      | CIAM Identity Provider for the wiki app        |
  | Authorization Server |                                |                                                |
  +----------------------+--------------------------------+------------------------------------------------+
  | Resource             | https://auth.feedback.example  | Authorization Server for a customer feedback   |
  | Authorization Server |                                | service                                        |
  +----------------------+--------------------------------+------------------------------------------------+
  | Resource Server      | https://api.feedback.example   | Public API for the customer feedback service   |
  +----------------------+--------------------------------+------------------------------------------------+
```

## Preconditions
- The First Party App has a registered OAuth 2.0 Client (`com.example.wiki`) with the CIAM IdP (`auth.wiki.example`)
- The First Party App has a registered OAuth 2.0 Client (`18723649238176`) with the Whitelabel AS (`auth.feedback.example`)
- Application Developer has established a trust relationship between their CIAM IdP and the First Party App for SSO
- Application Developer has established a trust relationship between their CIAM IdP and the Whitelabel AS for SSO and Identity Assertion JWT Authorization Grant
- Application Developer has granted the First Party App permission to act on behalf of users within the Whitelabel API with a specific set of scopes

## Example Sequence

### First Party App establishes a User Identity with CIAM IdP

#### IdP Authorization Request (with PKCE)
First Party App generates a PKCE code_verifier and a code_challenge (usually a SHA256 hash of the verifier, base64url-encoded) and redirects the end-user to the CIAM IdP with an authorization request
```
GET /authorize?
  response_type=code
  &client_id=com.example.wiki
  &redirect_uri=https://wiki.example/oauth2/callback
  &scope=openid+profile+email
  &state=xyzABC123
  &code_challenge=E9Melhoa2OwvFrEMTJguCHaoeK1t8URWbuGJSstw-cM
  &code_challenge_method=S256
Host: auth.wiki.example
```
No explicit user authorization step is required as the application is considered first party. 
The CIAM IdP authenticates the end-user and redirects back to the First Party App's registered client redirect URI with an authorization code:

    https://wiki.example/oauth2/callback?code=SplxlOBeZQQYbYS6WxSbIA&state=xyzABC123

First Party App exchanges the code and PKCE code_verifier to obtain an ID Token and Access Token for the IdP's UserInfo endpoint

```
POST /oauth2/token
Host: auth.wiki.example
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

grant_type=authorization_code
&code=SplxlOBeZQQYbYS6WxSbIA
&redirect_uri=https://wiki.example/oauth2/callback
&client_id=com.example.wiki
&code_verifier=dBjftJeZ4CVP-mB92K27uhbUJU1p1r_wW1gFWFOEjXk

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json

{
"id_token": "eyJraWQiOiJzMTZ0cVNtODhwREo4VGZCXzdrSEtQ...",
"token_type": "Bearer",
"access_token": "7SliwCQP1brGdjBtsaMnXo",
"scope": "openid profile email"
}
```
First Party App validates the ID Token using the published JWKS for the IdP
```
{
"iss": "https://auth.wiki.example/",
"sub": "1997e829-2029-41d4-a716-446655440000",
"aud": "com.example.wiki",
"exp": 1984444800,
"iat": 1684441200,
"auth_time": 1684440000,
"name": "John Connor",
"email": "john.connor@cyberdyne.example",
"email_verified": true
}
```

###  First Party App calls Whitelabeled API

As the First Party App is integrated directly with the Whitelabeled API, no AS discovery step is necessary.
The App can proceed directly to requesting an ID-JAG for token exchange.

#### First Party App obtains an Identity Assertion JWT Authorization Grant for Whitelabeled API from the CIAM IdP
First Party App makes an ID-JAG token exchange request to the CIAM IdP. It presents the ID Token obtained in the previous step, along with the scopes and resource identifier for the whitelabel API (both preconfigured by the developer)
```
POST /oauth2/token HTTP/1.1
Host: auth.wiki.example
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

grant_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:token-exchange
&requested_token_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:id-jag
&audience=https://auth.feedback.example/
&resource=https://api.feedback.example/
&scope=feedback.submit
&subject_token=eyJraWQiOiJzMTZ0cVNtODhwREo4VGZCXzdrSEtQ...
&subject_token_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:id_token
&client_assertion_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:client-assertion-type:jwt-bearer
&client_assertion=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6IjIyIn0...
```
If access is granted, the CIAM IDP creates a signed Identity Assertion JWT Authorization Grant and returns it in the token exchange response.
```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json
Cache-Control: no-store
Pragma: no-cache

{
"issued_token_type": "urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:id-jag",
"access_token": "eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsI...",
"token_type": "N_A",
"scope": "feedback.submit",
"expires_in": 300
}
```
Identity Assertion JWT Authorization Grant claims:
```
{
"alg": "ES256",
"typ": "oauth-id-jag+jwt"
}
.
{
"jti": "9e43f81b64a33f20116179",
"iss": "https://auth.wiki.example",
"sub": "1llb-b4c0-0000-8000-t800b4ck0000",
"aud": "https://auth.feedback.example",
"resource": "https://api.feedback.example/",
"client_id": "18723649238176",
"exp": 1984445160,
"iat": 1984445100,
"scope": "feedback.submit"
}
.
signature
```
#### First Party App obtains an Access Token for Whitelabel API
First Party App makes a token request to the previously registered whitelabel API's Authorization Server token endpoint using the Identity Assertion JWT Authorization Grant obtained from the CIAM IDP as a JWT Assertion.

The First Party App authenticates with its client credentials that were registered with the Whitelabel API's Authorization Server

```
POST /oauth2/token HTTP/1.1
Host: auth.feedback.example
Authorization: Basic yZS1yYW5kb20tc2VjcmV0v3JOkF0XG5Qx2

grant_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:jwt-bearer
assertion=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsI...
```
Whitelabel API's Authorization Server validates the Identity Assertion JWT Authorization Grant using the published JWKS for the trusted CIAM IDP
```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json;charset=UTF-8
Cache-Control: no-store
Pragma: no-cache

{
"token_type": "Bearer",
"access_token": "2YotnFZFEjr1zCsicMWpAA",
"expires_in": 86400,
"scope": "feedback.submit"
}
```
#### First Party App makes an authorized Whitelabel API request
First Party App invokes a Whitelabel API provided by the Resource Server with a valid access token
```
POST /feedback/submit
Host: api.feedback.example
Authorization: Bearer 2YotnFZFEjr1zCsicMWpAA"
Accept: application/json

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json

{
...
}
```

items.title: Audience vs Resource Clarity
items.body: We made the choice to require `audience` to be the Resource AS identifier and `resource` to be the protected resource for the Resource AS in this spec for interoperability.  While this approach seems allowed from how the Token Exchange spec is written, it isn't obvious to some readers who are familiar with Token Exchange.  We are effective profiling `audience` as the trust domain identifier vs defining a new param.  We don't need to change our decision but I think it may help to better state why we made this choice and provide some examples of multi-tenant resource authorization servers to help the reader understand how this profile is using audience and resource. Note that audience vs resource is a historically confusing problem across the specs.

items.title: Why don't the IdP requests the access token from the AS?
Authorization Server Metadata to enable discovery of JWT Assertion Grant
items.body:
A given client currently as no mechanism to discover whether an AS supports `oauth-id-jag+jwt` JWT as a valid JWT bearer grant type (`urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:jwt-bearer`)

(This was split off from #16 to focus on only the Resource App discovery question)

items.title: The spec states that `refresh_token` SHOULD NOT be used
items.body:
The spec currently states that, `refresh_token` SHOULD NOT be used. I also saw that there is an old closed issue with the comment:
> Instead we'll recommend using the refresh token to get a new ID token.

Is that decision final, because I do have some security concerns with using `refresh_token` for an `id-jag` and it also seems to be discouraged by RFC 8693, that states:
> A refresh token will typically not be issued when the exchange is of one temporary credential (the subject_token) for a different temporary credential (the issued token) for use in some other context.

items.title: Consisten capitalisation of Call Chain and Trust Domain
items.body: Do a editorial pass to consistently use capitalisations for those terms.

items.title: action_subject: The specific entity this transaction acts on
items.body:
Problem Statement
A token says what action to perform but not on which specific entity — leaving every service to figure that out independently.

Simple scenario
A loan approval token says action: approveLoan. But there are 50 pending loans. Which one? The loan service has to look up whose loan this is, cross-reference the agent's session, and guess.
More importantly: for shared resources (a document multiple agents can access, a shared account), saying resource: approveloan doesnt justify. The token must say this specific loan, for this specific applicant.

Proposed claim name: action_subject
"tctx": {
"action": "approveLoan",
"action_subject": {
"entity_type": "loan_application",
"entity_id": "loan-8821",
"entity_ref": "[alex.turner@acme.com](mailto:alex.turner@acme.com)"
}
}

A2A examples:
"entity_ref": "agent:financial-analyst-workload"
"entity_ref": "workload:fraud-detection-service-v2"
"entity_ref": "urn:dataset:fraud-training-v3"
"entity_ref": "urn:device:sensor-prod-042"

Taxonomy:
entity_type — what class of thing.
entity_id — unique ID within that class.
entity_ref — how to address or resolve it in the target system.

items.title: "Downscoping" transaction tokens to particular target workloads
items.body:
In Transaction Tokens, the `aud` claim represents the entire trust domain, which is opposite to OAuth, where it reflects individual resource servers. There is no claim defined that would represent particular workloads as the receivers for the transaction token.

In the absence of the `aud` equivalent having workload-level granularity, how do we implement the "strict audience check" concept from OAuth?

Example: a workload obtains a TraT with audience set to the entire trust domain. Under some circumstances, it might decide to "downscope" the TraT (via replacement request), limiting its "audience" to only selected workloads inside the trust domain, and thus enforcing only a specific request propagation path.

(Credits: Isabella Dix @ Keycloak Dev Day 2026)

items.title: List alg as REQUIRED parameter for both Client Attestation JWT and PoP JWT
Allow public client registration at the Resource Authorization Server when the client is confidential at the IdP and the ID-JAG is key-bound?
items.body:
The current draft generally assumes confidential clients at the Resource Authorization Server (RAS), and the Security Considerations say that this specification `SHOULD` only be supported for confidential clients. The draft also notes in Cross-Domain Client ID Handling that requiring a confidential client at the RAS helps prevent the IdP from delegating access to any valid client at the RAS.

At the same time, the draft now defines proof-of-possession / key-binding for ID-JAG using `cnf` and DPoP. That raises a question about whether the spec should allow an explicit tradeoff here.

**Deployment model to discuss**

- The client is confidential at the IdP.
- The client is public at the RAS, or the RAS otherwise does not require confidential client authentication for this exchange.
- The client includes DPoP in the token exchange to the IdP.
- The IdP issues a key-bound ID-JAG with a `cnf` / `jkt`.
- The client presents DPoP when exchanging the ID-JAG at the RAS.
- The RAS verifies that the DPoP key matches the `cnf` in the ID-JAG.

**Why allow this**

- Credential lifecycle can be centralized at the IdP.
- The client does not need to maintain separate credentials with each RAS.
- This may be useful in ecosystems with many downstream RAS deployments.
- Client ID Metadata Document (CIMD) helps address part of this problem when adopted, but it will not always be available immediately.
- This could make the deployment model possible until CIMD becomes more widely deployed.

**Tradeoff**

This is not equivalent to requiring a confidential client at the RAS.

What is preserved:

- Replay resistance for the ID-JAG, assuming the RAS requires proof-of-possession and validates `cnf` against the proof token.
- Stronger protection than an unconstrained bearer-style exchange.

What is reduced:

- The RAS no longer gets the same client-authentication assurance it gets from a confidential registration at the RAS.
- The anti-client-substitution property is weaker unless the spec explicitly defines how public-client identification is handled in this profile.

**Question**

Should the specification explicitly allow this tradeoff?

Possible direction:

- Keep confidential registration at the RAS as the recommended/default model.
- Allow RAS deployments to accept public clients for ID-JAG exchange when the ID-JAG is key-bound and the proof token presented to the RAS matches the `cnf`.
- Clarify that this does not require any RAS to support public clients.
- A RAS, or the administrator/operator of that RAS, can always decide to require confidential registration instead.
- In that sense, this change would make the model possible, not mandatory, until CIMD is more widely deployed.

If the spec does allow this, it would likely need explicit processing rules for this profile, for example:

- `cnf` in the ID-JAG is required.
- Proof-of-possession at the RAS is required.
- The proof key MUST match the `cnf` in the ID-JAG.
- The RAS SHOULD issue only sender-constrained access tokens in this case.
- The client identification / `client_id` matching rules need to be defined for public clients at the RAS.

The main question is whether the spec should permit this as an optional deployment tradeoff, while still allowing any RAS to require confidential registration.

items.title: The terminology in section 3 should be changed
items.body:
The current terminology is as follows:

>    Client Attestation JWT:  A JSON Web Token (JWT) generated by the
>       Client Attester which is bound to a key managed by a Client
>       Instance which can then be used by the instance for client
>       authentication.
> 
>    Client Attestation Proof of Possession (PoP) JWT:  A Proof of
>       Possession generated by the Client Instance using the key that the
>       Client Attestation JWT is bound to.
> 
>    Client Instance:  A deployed instance of a piece of client software.
> 
>    Client Instance Key:  A cryptographic asymmetric key pair that is
>       generated by the Client Instance where the public key of the key
>       pair is provided to the Client Attester.  This public key is then
>       encapsulated within the Client Attestation JWT and is utilized to
>       sign the Client Attestation Proof of Possession.
> 
>    Client Attester:  An entity that authenticates a Client Instance and
>       attests it by issuing a Client Attestation JWT.
> 
>    Challenge:  A String that is the input to a cryptographic challenge-
>       response pattern.  This is traditionally called a nonce within
>       OAuth.
> 

I propose the following rewording:

>    Client Attestation JWT:  A JSON Web Token (JWT) generated by the
>       Client Attester which is bound to a key managed by a Client
>       Instance which can then be used by the instance to support either 
>       an authentication service or a data origin authentication service.
> 
>    Client Attestation Proof of Knowledge (PoK) JWT:  A Proof of
>       Knowledge of the private key of a Client Instance Key generated 
>       by the Client Instance using that private key.
> 
>    Client Instance:  A deployed instance of a piece of client software
>       running on a type of device.
> 
>    Client Instance Key:  A cryptographic asymmetric key pair where the 
>       public key of the key pair is contained in a Client Attestation JWT 
>       and thus certified by the Client Attester.  
> 
>    Client Attester:  An entity that certifies a Client Instance and
>       attests that certification by issuing a Client Attestation JWT.
> 
>    Challenge (Opt):  A String that is the input to a cryptographic 
>       challenge-response pattern.
>

items.title: The Figure 1 should be redrawn
items.body:
The stages (1) to (4) are out of the scope of the document and should be renamed (a) to (f). See issue #157.

The flow description is missing to illustrate the ability of the AS to send a challenge to the Client Instance.

Let us number the exchanges between the Client and the AS starting with (1) which will be an optional flow.

This would change the description of the flows in the following way:

>    (1) The Client Instance sends an optional a challenge request to the
>        Authorisation Server.
> 
>    (2) If a challenge request has been sent to the Authorisation Server,
>        the Authorisation Server sends back a challenge to the Client 
>        Instance.
> 
>    (3) The Client Instance generates a Client Attestation PoK JWT with 
>        a Client Instance Key that includes the challenge and can include 
>        the hash value of a previous message exchanged with the 
>        Authorisation Server (e.g., a set of claims that the Client 
>        Instance has requested to be included into a credential that 
>        will be issued or a token request).
> 
>    (4) The Client Instance sends both the Client Attestation JWT and the
>        Client Attestation PoK JWT to the authorization server. 
> 
>    (5) The Authorisation Server verifies both the Client Attestation JWT 
>        and the Client Attestation PoK JWT and verifies the technical 
>        characteristics about the type of the device and the firmware / 
>        software on which the client instance is running match with its 
>        policy to issue either a set of claims that the Client Instance 
>        has requested to be included into a credential that will be 
>        issued or a security token.
> 

Note: PoK = Proof of Knowledge


```
             (c)
          +-------+
          |       |
          |      \ /
   +-----------------+      +-----------------+
   |                 |      |                 |
   | Client Attester |  OR  | Client Attester |
   |                 |      |                 |
   +-----------------+      +-----------------+
              / \  |          |
          (b)  |   |  (d)     | (f)
               |  \ /        \ /       (1) Opt.
             +-------------------+  chall. request   +---------------+
             |                   |-----------------> |               |
             |      Client       |(2) Opt. challenge | Authorization |
      +----->|     Instance      |<----------------- |     Server    |
  (a) |      |                   | (4) Response      |               |
      +------|                   |------------------>|               |
             +-------------------+                   +---------------+
                  / \      |                            / \      |
                   |       |                             |       |
                   +-------+                             +-------+  
                      (3)                                   (5)

```

items.title: The Introduction should be reworded
items.body:
The introduction states:

>    Traditional OAuth security concepts perform client authentication
>    through a backend channel.  In ecosystems such as the Issuer-Holder-
>    Verifier model used in [SD-JWT], this model raises privacy concerns,
>    as it would enable the backend to recognize which Holder (i.e.
>    client) interacts with which Issuer (i.e. Authorization Server) and
>    potentially furthermore see the credentials being issued.  

I have a problem with the wordings "backend channel", "the backend", and "frontend channel". 
Is a "client backend" identical to a "backend channel" ? I don't think so.

In the document history, there is:

>    -05
> 
> (...)
> 
>    *  rename client backend to client attester

Apparently, this renaming has not been fully done.
 
If "the backend" means "Client Attester", then the second sentence would become:

In ecosystems such as the Issuer-Holder-Verifier model used in [SD-JWT], this model raises privacy concerns, 
as it would enable the Client Attester to recognize which Holder (i.e. client) interacts with which Issuer (i.e. Authorization Server) 
and potentially furthermore see the credentials being issued.

However, it is doubtful that it is what was intended to be said.

I would believe that the whole paragraph should rather be changed into:

In ecosystems such as the Issuer-Holder-Verifier model used in [SD-JWT], this model raises privacy concerns, 
as it can enable Authorization Servers to recognize which Holder (i.e. client) interacts with other Authorization Servers (i.e., Issuers).

The text continue with:

>    This primary purpose of this specification is the authentication of a
>    client instance enabled through the client backend attesting to it.
>    The client backend may also attest further technical properties about
>    the hardware and software of the client instance.

As already mentioned in an earlier issue (see issue #155), Remote ATtestation procedureS (RATS) Architecture RFC 9334 states in Section 11:

> 11.  Privacy Considerations
> 
> (...)
> 
>    In many cases, the whole point of attestation procedures is to
>    provide reliable information about the type of the device and the
>    firmware/software that the device is running.

I would believe that the whole paragraph should be changed into:

>    This primary purpose of this specification is the authentication of a
>    client instance to an Authorisation Server, through the use of a 
>    Client Attester, allowing to attest technical characteristics about the 
>    type of the device and the firmware/software on which the client 
>    instance is running.

items.title: Changes in Client Keys can happen for non-nefarious reasons
items.body:
The text in https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-oauth-client-id-metadata-document-00#name-changes-in-client-keys suggests that a change in the content of the jwks_uri might indicate a condition that would need some kind of remediation or proactive disruption of something. 

However the key rotation dance in OIDC at https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-core-1_0.html#RotateSigKeys and https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-core-1_0.html#RotateEncKeys has the possibility of changes in the content of the jwks_uri as a normal course of events. And while that is in OIDC and this isn't OIDC, the pattern has permeated into the OAuth sphere. I think that if some of the actions from https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-oauth-client-id-metadata-document-00#name-changes-in-client-keys were taken, it would be detrimental to interop. 

Can this draft tone down the suggestions in Changes in Client Keys?

items.title: Proposal for Tx token initiation in agentic world
items.body:
### Problem Statement

Authorization systems based on zero trust principles must rethink their approach when dealing with AI agents, as the characteristics that differentiate AI agents from traditional web services have major security implications. Unlike static web services, AI agents can act autonomously, adapt their behavior, and access or integrate with new data sources dynamically—sometimes making decisions outside of their originally intended scope. This means that existing zero trust models, which tightly define permissions, verification processes, and access scopes for regular web services, may fall short when applied to adaptive, context-aware AI agents. For true zero trust security, authorization systems should treat each AI agent interaction as potentially unique, inspect agent requests with greater granularity, and continuously evaluate both intent and context—not just identity. Dynamic policy enforcement, ongoing behavioral analysis, and robust audit mechanisms become critical to ensure that AI agents do not inadvertently gain unauthorized access or act outside governance boundaries, despite using the same foundational security model as for traditional web services.

Transaction Tokens are a solution to propagate security context across the call graph in the form of cryptographically signed tokens. These tokens start at the front of the call chain and are propagated through all services in the call chain. Services can leverage the security context for access control and auditing. 

To support AI agent applications in the service graph, Transaction Tokens need to address several challenges: where and how tokens will be generated, how context inputs are captured from agents, scoping/limiting the contexts for agents, ensuring token propagation, and updating the auditing process to capture agent identity along with original actor details.
Agents by default must be given zero permissions and must be able to get permissions only when delegated by human user. Unsupervised with no human in loop agents could get permissions based on the task but sensitive operations should have human in loop to approve. 

Agents could be in different environments including developer’s machine (unsafe environment) or hosted in CloudAuth with protected access.


### Assumptions
 
Before jumping into solution, an assumption is being made here that OAuth will be used for agentic interactions. Agents will have no permissions of their own but will request permissions from Authorization Server (AS) with human in the loop consenting it. Agents will basically have access token that calls the human user as subject and agent as ‘actor; representing that agent is acting on behalf of the user.

For multi-agent, another assumption is that each agent will do OAuth Token Exchange RFC 8693 for getting access token which indicates that the agent is acting on behalf of the previous agent. These access tokens will contain delegation chain information about all agent identities.

### Why not initiate Transaction tokens at the agents ?  

The obvious solution to ensure Transaction token coverage across entire call chain starting from agent is to initiate Transaction token at beginning of call chain, in this case, it will be the Agentic application. The agentic application will have OAuth access token to access a resource server, but before calling resource server (or workload), it will request Transaction token using the access token. Transaction Token Issuer (TTI) will issue Tx token by validating the input access token. The input access token will have information about agent and human identity. It will also prove that agent is acting on behalf of human. 

Challenges with initiating token at agents : 
There are multiple challenges in this approach. The agent to agent or agent to MCP server interaction can be long running and Transaction tokens will expire. So we have to figure out mechanism to keep Tx token refrehsed. Token propagation is another challenge over MCP/A2A and other protocols. The agents could be running in browser or relatively low secure environments such as a human user’s laptop which increases the risk of exposing our bearer Transaction token.


### Proposal: Initiate transaction tokens once agentic interaction is over. 

The core idea of shifting token generation away from potentially unsafe environments and onto a trusted server is compelling. Core design principle - The proposal simplifies agent logic by shifting complexity to the trusted, centralized infrastructure—the Authorization Server and the Transaction Token Issuer. 

Let's take this flow as an example - 

```
Human -> Agent A → MCP Server → Resource server → Internal Service (Resource Server)
```

- Human will invoke Agent. 
- Agent will take action by calling MCP Server. 
- MCP Server throws OAuth challenge 401 unauthorized. 
- Agent which is already registered with OAuth Authorization Server (AS) will request access token. 
- AS will invoke authorization code flow and ask human for consent. 
- Human approves it and then standard auth code flow starts. At the end, agent has access token that says agent is allowed to take action on behalf of subject (human). 
- MCP Server gets the access token. 
- MCP server has to call internal service now. MCP Server gets access token from agent, and then invoke OAuth Token Exchange RFC 8693 with AS and gets another access token. 
- AS verifies permissions of MCP server, access tokens and then issues access token to MCP Server. 
- This access token contains delegation chain including (human, agent, MCP Server). 
- 
- MCP Server now has access token with all delegation chain and about to call internal service. 
- This is where it should initiate Tx token by calling  Transaction Token Issuer (TTI) and passing the access token. 
- TTI will have all the necessary information to add in the Tx token using the access token as the access token has call chain information. 
- TTI must validate the access token before trusting it for generating Transaction token.
- The generated Transaction token will have the human identity as the Subject, the first agent identity as 'act' claim. It also aligns with https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/modelcontextprotocol/issues/214 


As supervised agents act on behalf of humans, its important to include their identity in the Transaction tokens. Whenever human triggers an agent, sub MUST contain human identity and there should be a new claim in the token to include identity of the agent that acts on behalf of the human user.


```
act: : OPTIONAL The "act" (actor) claim, within the context of OAuth 2.0 token exchange and JSON Web Tokens (JWTs), is defined in RFC 8693: OAuth 2.0 Token Exchange. The value will be the agent that is acting on behalf of the human user. In case the access token contains delegation chain, then it will be the first actor in 'act' field where this on behalf of flow started.
```

### Detailed explanation of the proposal
The core idea is that Transaction Tokens will leverage the OAuth Authorization Server-issued access tokens as proof to issue a Transaction Token. These access tokens will be issued to AI agent applications and MCP servers in the call chain. For agent-to-agent or agent-to-MCP server interactions, access tokens from agents all the way to the last MCP server/agent will carry delegation chain information. This means all identities involved up to that point will be present in these access tokens. This differs from the current service access token model where an access token from Service A to Service B is different from an access token issued by Service B to call Service C. 

The advantage of this approach is that now Tx tokens aren’t exposed to unsafe environments in which agents are running. If agents do have long running interactions, they will still maintain access tokens because most of the agents are supposed to be OAuth compliant. This gives Tx token RFC to be popular and get widely adopted but still not blocked on agents support Tx token. 
).

For mid-call chain use cases, 
```
Workload A(w/ Tx token) → MCP Server A → MCP Server B → Workload-2
```
where Transaction Token propagation through the service graph starts but agent-to-agent interaction begins, the first MCP server or agent  (MCP Server A in above example) will perform an OAuth token exchange to get onBehalfOf permissions, and the Transaction Token context could be preserved in the access token as a claim. So between MCP Server A → MCP Server B, access token is flowing but this access token was requested by MCP Server A as part of OAuth token exchange. In OAuth token exchange, MCP Server should send access token and Tx token as well to ensure access token captures the Tx token contents as claim. Before calling Workload-2, the access token can be used to get a Transaction Token at MCP Server B. TTI this time, instead of generating a token with new contexts, the preserved contexts from access token claim will be used. 

In summary, access tokens will be leveraged to procure Transaction Tokens. The OAuth token exchanged access tokens contain a delegation chain of identities, and when agent interaction starts in the middle of the call chain, access tokens will preserve Transaction Token contexts to get Transaction Tokens back when service interaction begins.

This approach for Transaction Tokens depends on OAuth Authorization Server support for OAuth token exchange, registering AI applications in the OAuth Authorization Server for identities, and delegation and permission models. The approach simplifies overall Transaction Token integration in this AI agent-based service graph as Transaction Tokens don't need to implement propagation over various protocols, or worry about issuing bearer tokens in unsafe environments such as developers' machines or browsers.

The agent-side space is rapidly evolving, and by moving Transaction Token generation to the server side, integration stays simple while the OAuth Authorization Server handles the complexity. This approach assumes that all relevant protocols are or will be supporting OAuth, including agent applications. This means that agent application frameworks that support OAuth should be sufficient for AI agent apps to start development and launch, instead of integrating Transaction Tokens explicitly in their agent code and further complicating it.

### Security and additional considerations

- I acknowledge that while the risk of exposing a Transaction Token is removed, the risk of a compromised OAuth Access Token remains. Advocate for mTLS as a non-negotiable security measure for all inter-service communication to protect against this.

- We should consider may be new, standardized claim to the oauth-transaction-tokens working group for transporting Transaction Token context within an access token. This ensures interoperability and prevents proprietary implementations.

- We must mandate that all audit trails for AI agent interactions include the full delegation chain (human, agent), along with all dynamic business context used to make a decision.

-

items.title: Cross-domain Transaction Tokens
items.body:
Imagine two trust domains (A and B), each having its own TTS, with an existing trust relationship. It is assumed that some workloads from the domain A have direct connectivity to their counterparts from domain B (potentially including respective TTSs).

Can we provide a guidance on how to implement the cross-domain setup?

Example: a workload from A, having obtained TraT-A, might decide to call TTS-A with `audience` set to B, and ask for a replacement TraT-B. In this case, TTS-A will need to propagate that call to TTS-B under the hood.
Alternatively, if workload A has connectivity to TTS-B, it could request a replacement TraT-B directly.

This has some conceptual overlap with Identity Chaining, but I think it could be implemented in a simpler way (not requiring an intermediate credential like ID-JAG and an extra leg).

(Credits: @tnorimat @ Keycloak)

items.title: Security considerations: subject_token validation
items.body:
In the case of `subject_token` being an external access token, we could recommend preforming a revocation check, in addition to the mandatory signature and expiration checks. From the discussion with @PieterKas :

> In terms of revoked tokens, it is up to the transaction token service if it checks for that, or how it obtains that information (there is an ever increasing number of ways to find out about revoked tokens, including SSF/CAEP, token status list and token introspection).

Might be worth adding the above to the Security Considerations section.

items.title: Interoperability gap: JIT provisioning and identity claim negotiation
items.body:
# Problem

Section 2.1 of the draft currently says:

> It is RECOMMENDED that the ID-JAG contain an `email` and/or `aud_sub` claim. The Resource Authorization Server MAY use these claims for account resolution or just-in-time (JIT) account creation... Additional Resource Authorization Server specific identity claims MAY be needed for account resolution or JIT account creation.

This language acknowledges the JIT problem but does not solve it. There is no interoperable mechanism for a Resource Authorization Server (RAS) to signal which identity claims it requires, or for a Client to convey that signal back to the IdP so it can include them in the ID-JAG. The result requires out-of-band coordination between every IdP/RAS pair, which undermines the composability goal of the spec.

## Specific failure modes

1. **What claims does the IdP put in the ID-JAG?** The spec provides a fixed optional set (`email`, `aud_sub`) but gives no mechanism for the RAS to express its requirements. IdPs must either over-include claims (privacy risk) or guess conservatively and leave JIT broken.

2. **What claims does the RAS need to JIT?** RAS-specific claims (department, cost center, license tier, custom identifiers) may not be OIDC standard claims. Even for OIDC-defined claims, different RASes need different subsets. There is no way to express this per-RAS, per-deployment requirement today.

3. **How does the IdP know to issue the right claims?** The IdP has no standardized signal from the Client about what the target RAS needs. The Client knows the RAS endpoint (from discovery) but cannot ask the IdP to tailor the ID-JAG for that RAS's JIT requirements.

4. **The UserInfo dead end.** When the RAS receives the ID-JAG it has no Access Token and cannot reach back to the IdP's UserInfo endpoint or any other IdP-hosted endpoint to fetch additional claims. The ID-JAG exchange must succeed or fail on the claims already present in the token. This is not a fixable limitation; it is a structural constraint of the grant flow.

# Proposed Solution

Standardize exactly three things.

## 1. Error code: `insufficient_identity_claims`

If the RAS determines that the identity assertion is cryptographically valid and the grant type is accepted, but the token does not contain sufficient identity claims to perform account resolution or JIT account creation, it SHOULD return an error response per RFC 6749 Section 5.2:

```
HTTP/1.1 400 Bad Request
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "error": "insufficient_identity_claims",
  "error_description": "The identity assertion is missing required claims for account resolution."
}
```

The name follows the `insufficient_*` pattern established by RFC 9470 (`insufficient_user_authentication`). It signals "valid but not enough", distinguishing this condition from `invalid_grant`, which would indicate the token itself is rejected. 

## 2. Error response parameter: `required_claims`

The error response SHOULD include a `required_claims` parameter: a space-separated list of the claim names the RAS requires, following the same format as `scope` (RFC 6749 Section 3.3):

```json
{
  "error": "insufficient_identity_claims",
  "error_description": "The identity assertion is missing required claims for account resolution.",
  "required_claims": "email department employee_number"
}
```

Space-separated is the right format here. Claim names are JSON member names and cannot contain spaces, so the delimiter is unambiguous. This is consistent with `scope` and avoids the framework-parsing ambiguity of a JSON array in a form-encoded body (cf. RFC 9396 `authorization_details`, which uses JSON because its values are structured objects; claim names are flat tokens and do not warrant that complexity).

`required_claims` is intentionally defined as a flat list of names. Value constraints, type hints, and schema references are deferred pending real deployment experience.

## 3. Token Exchange request extension parameter: `required_claims`

A Client receiving an `insufficient_identity_claims` error MAY retry the Token Exchange request to the IdP Authorization Server, including the `required_claims` parameter with the value carried directly from the RAS error response:

```
POST /oauth2/token
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

grant_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:token-exchange
&requested_token_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:id-jag
&subject_token=<id_token>
&subject_token_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:id_token
&audience=https://api.example.com/
&required_claims=email+department+employee_number
```

The symmetric naming (`required_claims` in both the error response and the Token Exchange request) means the Client can pass the value through without transformation. "Required" reflects the RAS's perspective, which is authoritative: these claims are required for the flow to succeed. It does not obligate the IdP to provide all of them; the IdP SHOULD include the requested claims subject to its own policy (consent, availability, privacy controls), and MAY decline to include specific claims.

## End-to-end flow

```
Client → IdP:    Token Exchange → ID-JAG (minimal claims)
Client → RAS:    Token Exchange with ID-JAG
RAS → Client:    400 insufficient_identity_claims
                     required_claims=email department employee_number
Client → IdP:    Token Exchange + required_claims=email department employee_number
IdP → Client:    ID-JAG with additional claims included
Client → RAS:    Token Exchange with enriched ID-JAG
RAS → Client:    200 OK, access_token
```

# Editorial: relax `email` and `aud_sub` to plain OPTIONAL

With `required_claims` providing the negotiation mechanism, the RECOMMENDED status of `email` and `aud_sub` in Section 2.1 should be downgraded to OPTIONAL. These claims become examples of what a RAS might request rather than a standing recommendation that every IdP include them. The existing guidance language should be replaced with a pointer to the `required_claims` mechanism as the interoperable way to express and negotiate claim requirements, and an example end-to-end flow demonstrating the retry pattern.

# Advanced use case: RAS Token Exchange to IdP

For deployments where the identity data needed cannot reasonably be pre-embedded in a signed token (because it is large, sensitive, or needs to be fresher than the ID-JAG allows), the RAS MAY perform its own Token Exchange: presenting the received ID-JAG as the subject token to the IdP's token endpoint and requesting an Access Token scoped to the IdP's identity APIs.

This covers the UserInfo-equivalent pattern without burdening the Client as intermediary, and without embedding what is effectively a profile API response inside the ID-JAG. It should be acknowledged in the spec as a valid deployment pattern with the IdP-side API contract left to a future profile. This keeps the core ID-JAG spec clean and avoids scope creep.

# Optional / future: claim requirements discovery at the RAS

To allow IdPs to import a RAS's claim requirements at onboarding time rather than waiting for a runtime error, the RAS could advertise the claims it needs for JIT provisioning via its Authorization Server metadata (RFC 8414), using a new metadata parameter scoped to this profile (to avoid collision with base OAuth or other profiles).

This is an optimization for IdP onboarding UX, not a correctness requirement. The runtime `insufficient_identity_claims` negotiation works without it. Worth raising for debate; not a blocker for the core proposal.

---

# Questions for the group

1. **Error name:** `insufficient_identity_claims` (cause, RFC 9470 pattern)  or alternative?
2. **IdP acknowledgment:** Should the IdP be required to indicate in the ID-JAG which claims from `required_claims` it was able to include, or is the Client expected to inspect the resulting token before retrying at the RAS?
3. **RAS-to-IdP Token Exchange:** Should the advanced pattern be noted in Security Considerations? The RAS acting as an OAuth client to the IdP has trust and SSRF implications worth calling out even if the pattern itself is out of scope.

items.title: Secdir (Rich Salz) review
items.body:
from https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/oauth/ONFT-2RYXGdoUTjck1pZFlHH07Q/ 
>
> The only issue I found was that there discussion of backward compatibility
> other than Section 3, where it's kinda weakly stated. The identifier isn't
> changing, so at least a statement that it is backward compatible would be
> helpful I think.


What is and isn't backward compatible here is a little subtle. A sender
doing the new thing will be accepted by a receiver doing the old thing and
by a receiver doing the new thing, so that's fully backward compatible. But
some variations of the sender doing the old thing would be rejected by a
receiver strictly doing the new thing, so that's the not entirely backward
compatible part that might need some migration consideration.

I'll make an effort to improve treatment of the subject in the draft. If,
after reading that rambling explanation from me, you have thoughts about
where or how in the draft to make such improvements though, I'd be happy to
hear suggestions.

items.title: Clarify cross-domain use restriction
items.body:
> This specification is intended for cross-domain uses where the Client, Resource App, and Identity Provider are all in different trust domains. In particular, the Identity Provider MUST NOT issue access tokens in response to an ID-JAG it issued itself. Doing so could lead to unintentional broadening of the scope of authorization.

This should not preclude a single piece of software from being both an IdP and Resource AS.

items.title: Section 4.1.1 should probably mention Pushed Authorization Requests
items.body:
Currently the text in [Section 4.1.1](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-ietf-oauth-v2-1-09.html#section-4.1.1) only mentions the parameters you would send to the `authorize` endpoint if you were not using PAR.

I think it'd be a wise to say that PAR replaces these parameters with just `request_uri` and (maybe) `client_id`.

items.title: Describe OAuth's use of application/x-www-form-urlencoded encoding

labels.description: Items to discuss in the next WG interim meeting

items.body:
499a8f88b70ca00bfa5ef588023c68c1d0277bff removed Appendix B but unlinked references to it are still within the document next to almost every `x-www-form-urlencoded` mention.

---

Throughout the years this Appendix was THE resource used over and over to explain why and how the OAuth use of the Basic authorization scheme encodes the username and password tokens.

If this Appendix is removed I would propose to add examples of client secret basic with username and password tokens where the client_id and client_secret encoding changes the octets that go into the basic authorization scheme base64 encoding. This is an often overlooked implementation detail that both client and server implementers get wrong and end up inoperable, further driving users to use client secret post which this document marks as NOT RECOMMENDED.

items.title: Mixup attacks with issuer identifiers
items.body:
Picture this scenario:

* `example.com` run an AS which implements this spec
* `example.net` also run an AS with issuer identifier `https://example.net/`. 
* `example.com`'s AS is also connected to `example.net` as a client 

If an attacker can place a JSON file at `https://example.net/`, they can use it as a client ID. They can then use the client credentials grant and potentially (depending upon which OAuth 2 extensions are implemented by `example.com`) will mint JWTs with `{"iss":"https://example.com","sub":"https://example.net"}`.They may be able to trigger flows which result in minting OpenID Connect ID tokens `{"iss":"https://example.com","aud":"https://example.net","sub":"usersub"}`. These ID tokens may then be abusable with e.g. the JWT bearer authorization grant, e.g. via the identity chaining spec. 

It may seem unlikely that an attacker can place a JSON file at the root of `https://example.net/`, but consider a setup where a frontend proxy has the configuration:

* `https://example.net/.well-known/` -> forwarded to IDP. The other endpoints of the IDP might live on e.g. `https://auth.example.com/`
* `https://example.net/` -> forwarded to a Wordpress installation with insecure plugins

This attack can be relatively silently executed. The attacker can modify the code at `example.net` to sniff if the request is coming from a web browser (inferable because the user agent starts with `Mozilla`, or because `text/html` is in the accept header, or similar) and return the usual home page, only returning the client ID metadata to clients that look like an AS.

There are three potential ways I see of avoiding this:

1. Embed the client metadata inside the AS metadata document. This makes the `oauth-authorization-server` name misleading (though `openid-connect` less so), but it absolutely guarantees the two entities with the same URI are actually the same entity (In retrospect it might have been better for it to be `/.well-known/oauth` with properties under e.g. `"as"`, `"rp"` and `"rs"`, but that ship sailed a long time ago)
2. Place the client metadata in `/.well-known/oauth-client` and trust that `.well-known` is well controlled. This is probably OK. There may be a hazard here between OAuth and OpenID style uses of `.well-known`...
3. Proceed as is, write several paragraphs of guidance in the Security Considerations section cautioning people about potential for confusion between client IDs and AS issuer IDs and watch as everyone gets it wrong and we create a new rich mine of security vulnerabilities

items.title: request_context handling
items.body:
The `request_context` parameter is used to pass security-sensitive information to the TTS. That means, the `request_context` parameter (or its individual fields?) should only be accepted from the requestors with an elevated level of trust.

For example, the only way to communicate the end-user's IP address to the TTS is via `request_context`. The only workload that could be trusted for that, is the Edge / API Gateway; other workloads do not possess this information, but could forge it, should the `request_context` parameter be accepted from them.

Should we add a paragraph on the special handling of `request_context`? Some other thoughts:
* should we ignore `request_context` in the case of `subject_token` being self-signed or unsigned (or, generally, being other than external access token)?
* as a consequence, should we ignore `request_context` in the case of TraT replacement request?

items.title: authn parameter
items.body: For the `authn` request parameter, only the example value of `urn:ietf:rfc:6749` is provided. Could we elaborate on the format / recognized values? For example, we mention SAML in 14.5 Subject Token Types. However, it is unclear what the value of `authn` should be in this case.

I am very aware that some other specs/profiles/whatever use issuer as part of a key discovery routine. But this isn't any of those specs/profiles/whatever so this **MUST** isn't meaningful.
